evals.prompt: We get NPS scores back every month but we're not really doing anything with them. Where do I start?
evals.expected_output: Pulls the distribution and existing flow performance. Identifies single-bucket treatment as the core problem. Recommends segmenting the response stream into promoter, passive, recoverable detractor, damage-control detractor, and cut-loose detractor with different treatment per segment.
assertions: Calls modern.surveys.nps_distribution for the distribution
Calls modern.flows.revenue_by_flow filtered to NPS-triggered flows
Identifies single-bucket treatment as the core problem
Names the five segments (promoter, passive, recoverable, damage-control, cut-loose)
Recommends per-segment treatment design
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: What should I do when a customer gives me a 2 out of 10 with a complaint about late shipping?
evals.expected_output: Identifies this as a recoverable detractor scenario with a clear addressable complaint. Recovery rate 40-60% when handled well. Recommends direct outreach within 24 hours, ownership, a meaningful gesture (refund, replacement, credit), and explicit follow-up. Distinguishes this from damage-control and cut-loose detractor handling.
assertions: Identifies this as a recoverable detractor scenario
Cites 40-60% recovery rate when handled well
Recommends direct outreach within 24 hours
Recommends a meaningful gesture (refund, replacement, credit)
Distinguishes from damage-control and cut-loose handling
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Should I send a winback offer to a customer who scored us a 3 last month?
evals.expected_output: Hard no unless the underlying complaint has been resolved. Names the do-not-contact rule for detractors with unresolved complaints. Walks through the resolution cycle (acknowledgment, action, follow-up confirmation) before any marketing send. Cross-references winback-flows do-not-contact set as mutually inclusive.
assertions: Recommends not sending the winback offer
Names the do-not-contact rule for detractors with unresolved complaints
Walks through the resolution cycle (acknowledgment, action, follow-up)
Cross-references winback-flows do-not-contact set
Names the spam-complaint and inbox-placement consequences
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: We have a lot of passives in the 7-8 range. They're not unhappy but they're not advocates. What do I do?
evals.expected_output: Walks through the passive activation framework. Brand-deepening content over generic discounts. Behind-the-scenes content, founder communication, product story, sourcing detail, community access. Discounts to passives are brand-flat. Names the 15-25 point passive-to-promoter shift target.
assertions: Recommends brand-deepening content over generic discounts
Names specific content types (BTS, founder, product story, sourcing, community)
Warns against generic discount as a passive activation tool
Cites the 15-25 point passive-to-promoter conversion target
Distinguishes passive treatment from detractor recovery
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: A customer just scored us a 10. We send a thank you email. Is that enough?
evals.expected_output: Identifies promoters as the most under-leveraged segment. Recommends a stronger ask: referral trigger immediately (4-8x average referral conversion at this moment), UGC ask with specific concrete prompt, case-study or advocacy invitation for high-AOV categories. References referral-program-design for design detail.
assertions: Identifies promoters as under-leveraged
Recommends referral trigger immediately on promoter response
Cites 4-8x average referral conversion at this moment
Recommends concrete UGC ask over generic thanks
Names case-study or advocacy path for high-AOV categories
References referral-program-design skill
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Our overall NPS dropped from 42 to 36 last quarter. What does that mean?
evals.expected_output: Notes that aggregate NPS hides cohort-level shifts. Pulls cohort NPS drift by acquisition cohort and channel before interpreting. Walks through the three typical causes (acquisition channel shift, product or formulation change, onboarding or expectation change). Names the 60-90 day lead before LTV signal follows.
assertions: Notes that aggregate NPS hides cohort-level shifts
Calls modern.surveys.nps_distribution grouped by cohort and channel
Walks through the three typical causes of drift
Names acquisition channel shift as the most frequent cause
Cites the 60-90 day lead before LTV signal follows
Recommends acting on cohort drift, not waiting for LTV deterioration
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: When should I send the NPS survey? Right after they get the product?
evals.expected_output: Walks through NPS collection timing framework. Day 0-3 is too early (no product experience). Day 14-30 is the right window for replenishment; day 30-60 for considered purchase. Day 90+ is too late. Recommends two-question form (score + open-text reason) as default.
assertions: Identifies day 0-3 as too early
Names day 14-30 right window for replenishment
Names day 30-60 right window for considered purchase
Names day 90+ as too late (recall decay)
Recommends two-question form (score + open-text) as default
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Do detractors actually churn more than promoters? How tightly does NPS predict retention?
evals.expected_output: Pulls churn rate and repeat rate grouped by NPS segment. Confirms detractors churn at meaningfully higher rates than promoters almost universally; the gap is the leading-indicator value of NPS. Walks through using cohort NPS at week 2 as a leading indicator for 90-day retention.
assertions: Calls modern.retention.churn_rate grouped by NPS segment
Calls modern.retention.repeat_rate grouped by NPS segment
Confirms detractors churn at higher rates
Names cohort NPS at week 2 as leading indicator for 90-day retention
Distinguishes leading-indicator use from lagging-indicator brand-health use
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections
